focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

The flour bin sat emptied into a washpan, and three jars of last summer's peaches lined the cellar step. The ledger still was not in the house.

She had looked where she had hidden it and where fear said a thief would look next, then in the foolish places a woman only searched when sleep was gone and sense with it: under the ticking of her mattress, behind the stove reservoir, in the coal hod, under the folded Sunday tablecloths that smelled of iron and starch and old smoke. The cellar shelf rasped her stiff thumb raw. Dust from the joists settled into her hair. Somewhere downriver the dam gave one of its low concussions and the peaches shivered against their glass, syrup trembling at their shoulders. Men who had never eaten from this pantry were counting down the valley's days.

Ruthie stood in the kitchen doorway with her sleeves rolled above her reddened wrists, not stopping Della and not helping either. That was worse. If Ruthie had scolded, Della could have braced against it. If she had joined in, Della could have mistaken that for agreement. Instead she watched with a dish towel pulled through both hands as though she might wring an answer out of the cloth.

"You'll break every jar in here before you bring a book back by force," she said.

"It was here."

"I know where it was." Ruthie glanced toward the open cellar hatch, then back at Della. "I know where it isn't."

Della set another jar on the floorboards harder than she meant to. The ring bit into her palm. Syrup glowed amber behind the glass, thick as old bruise. "Lucille's note said eight."

"It ain't eight yet."

The note lay on the table under the sugar bowl, a half sheet of ruled paper in Lucille Holt's compact hand: Need you in Larkin this morning. County car can take you if ready by eight. U...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...roof tin, the sound filling the pauses the way gossip had filled the cemetery. She slid the child buttons into the pocket of her dress and looked out toward the valley, where Old Sion sat hidden now behind the trees and weather, its children's row scraped empty by afternoon labor and left to the storm.

Della stood beside her with mud drying on her skirt and old dirt stiff in the cracks of her hands. Down at the cemetery they had finished the removals before the weather broke. They had come there to do exactly that, and they had done it. By supper the child section would be all tagged boxes under canvas instead of names under cedar. By nightfall Pearl Lusk would have the twin story turned mean enough to travel every porch between Cedar Bench and the river.

June did not cry. She hooked two fingers over the tag book's spine and worried the cloth until it wrinkled under her nail, and that, more than anything, made Della understand how far the stain had already spread. Tears would have kept the thing in the lane with Lem Harbin and Nettie Crowe. Instead June stood there dry-eyed and straight-backed, already braced for schoolyard questions, churchyard stares, and the next porch woman who decided not to bring Ruthie her mending.

Della took the book out of June's hand and went inside with it. She set it beside the lamp on the kitchen table, crossed to the cellar door, and lifted the latch until the swollen wood gave a little under her palm. Ruthie had gone still at the sink behind her.

"After supper," Della said, looking at the door and not at either of them, "I'm bringing the ledger up. Only the grave pages. I'm done letting this valley invent the rest."

The rain thickened. Somewhere downriver the dam sounded again, faint through weather and distance, still keeping time.
